title: RL-Seq: reading the solvent accessibility of RNA with hydroxyl radicals

blocks.text: If you only look at that table of cleavage counts laid out by position, RL-Seq is easily taken for a structure detector that "determines pairing base by base". That reading is wrong. The object of interpretation for RL-Seq is the solvent accessibility of the RNA backbone in solution, and the way it reads out accessibility carries a very distinctive chemical signature: the hydroxyl radical (•OH) generated by the Fenton reaction cleaves the solvent-exposed backbone, leaving a **3′-phosphate end**, and RL-Seq relies precisely on **RtcB ligase to join the 5′-OH end of a fixed rM13 adapter to this 3′-P break point** to transcribe "where it was cleaved" into sequenceable fragments. In other words, what RL-Seq reports is "whether a stretch of backbone faces the solvent and is therefore cleaved into a break point that can be ligation-captured", not "this base must be paired" or "must be unpaired".

This boundary is crucial for FoldBridge. A break point captured by a single RtcB ligation is essentially a solvent-accessibility event, not a pairing call. FoldBridge can preserve the cleavage counts as is, and it can display these ligation-captured readouts on the web; but as soon as they are to enter default structure colouring, interpretive text, or confidence, they must first answer a more specific question: within the chemistry and readout context of "•OH cleavage + 3′-P ligation capture", can this number count as evidence of backbone solvent accessibility? The raw cleavage signal must first pass through its own ligation/library context before it is eligible to become a candidate for default colour or confidence.
The experimental chain: the Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals, RtcB joins a fixed adapter

blocks.legend: **Figure 2. The main experimental steps of RL-Seq.** This figure from Solayman et al. 2022 (PMID 36369947, DOI `10.1080/15476286.2022.2145098`) takes the experimental chain of RL-Seq apart step by step: (1) hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are generated in vitro by the Fenton reaction, used to probe RNA or RNA-protein complexes; (2) solvent-exposed regions (the blue arrows in the figure) are cleaved by •OH, generating a 3′-phosphate (3′-P) end, while buried regions cannot reach the •OH (the black dots in the figure) and are not cleaved; (3) the 3′-P end of the cleaved fragment is joined by RtcB ligase to the 5′-OH end of the rM13 adapter; (4) the purified ligation product is chemically fragmented, and fragments of 150–200 nt are selected; (5) cDNA is synthesised by reverse transcription using an adapter-specific primer; (6) sequencing-platform-specific adapters are added to the 5′ and 3′ ends by PCR, for high-throughput sequencing; (7) the sequencing data are analysed.
body: In reading this flow chart, the one point most worth remembering is this: the signal of RL-Seq comes from "whether a break point can be joined by RtcB", so what it measures is the solvent accessibility of the backbone (hydroxyl radical footprinting), not the base pairing state itself. A stretch that is cleaved a lot, with many ligation-captured fragments, indicates that this stretch of backbone is exposed to the solvent; a stretch that is cleaved little, or where no break point can even be read, may be a backbone that is buried, wrapped in by tertiary structure folding, or shielded by a protein/ligand, or it may simply be that the 3′-P end of that stretch was not efficiently joined.

For FoldBridge, this means that the numbers in the final table are not a "structural label" given directly by •OH, but a measured quantity arrived at after cleavage, RtcB ligation, library preparation, reverse transcription, sequencing, and normalisation. This measured quantity is very useful, but what it reports is solvent exposure, and it must first be interpreted within the chemical context of RL-Seq before one can talk about structure.
blocks.label: RL-Seq F2
blocks.legendText: The main experimental steps of RL-Seq method . (1) Hydroxyl radicals ( • OH) were generated from the Fenton reaction for probing RNA or RNA-protein complex in vitro . (2) Solvent exposed areas (blue arrow) are cleaved by reacting with • OH to create 3′-phosphate (3′-P) ends, whereas buried regions are not accessible to • OH (black dot). (3) The 3′-P ends of cleaved fragments were ligated with the 5′-OH ends of the rM13 linker by RtcB ligase. (4) The purified ligated products are fragmented chemically and selected for those between 150 and 200 nt long. (5) A linker-specific primer was used to synthesized cDNA by reverse transcriptase enzyme. (6) Sequencing platform-specific adapters are added at 5′ and 3′ ends by PCR reaction for high-throughput sequencing. (7) Sequencing data analysis.

blocks.text: Cleavage intensity needs optimising: both over-cleavage and under-cleavage distort the signal

blocks.legend: **Figure 3. Optimising the hydroxyl radical cleavage intensity of ribosomal RNA.** The legend of Solayman et al. 2022 (PMID 36369947, DOI `10.1080/15476286.2022.2145098`) points out that over-cleavage can be caused by an RNA concentration that is too low, an H₂O₂ concentration that is too high, or an incubation time that is too long. (A) Based on previous studies, the authors fixed H₂O₂ at 0.6% and the incubation time at 1 minute, then progressively increased the amount of RNA used (total 0.5–4 µg); the Urea-PAGE gel shows that the higher the RNA concentration, the lower the cleavage proportion. (B) A total of 3.5 µg of rRNA was finally determined as the optimal concentration for hydroxyl radical probing. In the figure, M is the 1kb-plus DNA ladder, Untreated indicates no •OH probing, and Treated indicates •OH probing.
body: This figure puts an easily overlooked safeguard on the table: the cleavage conditions directly determine what the raw signal looks like. If cleavage goes too far, almost every site is cleaved, and the difference between accessible and inaccessible is erased; if cleavage is insufficient, the exposure signal that should have appeared cannot be read out. The accessibility conclusion must rest on controlled cleavage (near single-hit).

For FoldBridge, this is exactly the concrete origin of "the raw observation must first pass through the readout context". The cleavage count at the same site does not mean the same thing under different cleavage intensities. Treating a number without an annotated cleavage condition directly as a backbone accessibility ground truth conflates the experimental parameters with the structural interpretation.
blocks.label: RL-Seq F3
blocks.legendText: Optimizing the intensity of hydroxyl radical cleavage of ribosomal RNA . Excessive cleavage was resulted from a low concentration of RNA, high concentration of H 2 O 2 or longer incubation time (not shown here). (A) According to the previous studies, we have fixed the concentration of H 2 O 2 at 0.6% and incubation time at 1 m, then increased the concentration of RNA (0.5–4 ug in total). The Urea-PAGE gel image showed that higher the concentration of RNA, the lower the cleavage ratio. (B) Finally, we have determined 3.5 ug rRNA in total as an optimum concentration for hydroxyl radical probing. Here, M: 1kb – plus DNA ladder, Untreated: Without • OH probing, and Treated: probing with • OH.

blocks.text: Mapping the signal onto the three-dimensional surface: strong signal corresponds to solvent-exposed backbone

blocks.legend: **Figure 5. Surface representation of 16S rRNA.** Solayman et al. 2022 (PMID 36369947, DOI `10.1080/15476286.2022.2145098`) (A) the ribosomal assembly extracted from PDB: 4V7T, with 16S rRNA coloured by RL-Seq signal; (B) a local close-up of residues 99–299 of 16S rRNA; (C) a rotated view of residues 99–299; (D) the chemical probing signal of residues 99–299 plotted as a bar chart at single-nucleotide resolution, overlaid with a line trace of the SASA extracted from PDB: 4V7T. This figure maps the RL-Seq signal onto the three-dimensional surface of 16S rRNA, showing that the signal corresponds to solvent-exposed backbone regions.
body: This figure puts the value of RL-Seq in the right place: when the cleavage signal is projected onto a real three-dimensional structural surface, the strong signal does indeed fall on the solvent-exposed (high SASA) backbone, and the overlay of the bar chart and the SASA line trace also visualises the "signal-accessibility" correspondence.

But this is precisely where misreading is easiest. The strong signal of RL-Seq corresponds to solvent-exposed backbone, not to a simple unpaired state. A stretch of backbone can sit within a paired helix yet still face the solvent and therefore be cleaved and ligation-captured very strongly; it can also be single-stranded yet wrapped inside by tertiary structure folding, with almost no break point readable. Accessibility is determined jointly by secondary structure and tertiary structure. For FoldBridge, translating a high RL-Seq signal directly into "this is unpaired" amounts to skipping the very layer it actually measured: the backbone faces the solvent, and the break point can be ligation-captured.
blocks.label: RL-Seq F5
blocks.legendText: Surface representation of 16S rRNA . (A) Ribosome assembly extracted from PDB: 4V7T with 16S rRNA coloured based on RL-Seq signal. (B) Close up image of 16S rRNA between residues 99–299. (C) Rotated image of 16S rRNA residues 99–299. (D) Bar plot of chemical probing signal at single nucleotide resolution between residues 99–299 with SASA extracted from PDB: 4V7T overlayed as a line plot.

blocks.text: Cross-validation is needed even between techniques of the same kind: RL-Seq vs HRF-Seq

blocks.legend: **Figure 6. The comparison of RL-Seq and HRF-Seq on 16S rRNA.** The legend of Solayman et al. 2022 (PMID 36369947, DOI `10.1080/15476286.2022.2145098`) is organised in three rows: the first row is nucleotide-level SASA, (A) evaluating the SCC (Spearman correlation coefficient) between SASA and •OH probing based on simulated sequencing depth, (B) the effect of probe size on SCC, (C) the effect of window size on SCC; the second row is ribose-level SASA, (D)(E)(F) corresponding respectively to the same evaluations of sequencing depth, probe size, and window size; the third row is atom-level SASA, (G)(H)(I) the same three evaluations. This figure compares RL-Seq and HRF-Seq on SASA, showing that cross-validation is also needed between accessibility methods of the same kind.
body: The significance of this figure is not "which method won", but a reminder: even when both are hydroxyl radical footprinting, the correlation of RL-Seq and HRF-Seq with SASA varies with sequencing depth, probe size, window size, and the definition of SASA at the different scales of nucleotide / ribose / atom.

For FoldBridge, the conclusion is very direct: there are differences even between accessibility techniques of the same kind, and one cannot treat just any accessibility number as equivalent structural evidence. An RL-Seq signal and an HRF-Seq signal, even if both point to "solvent accessible", may differ in their probe size, smoothing window, and reference SASA scale. Stuffing them into the same confidence channel without distinction makes the meaning of cross-validation disappear.
blocks.label: RL-Seq F6
blocks.legendText: Comparison between RL-Seq and HRF-Seq of 16S rRNA . First row: Nucleotide-level SASA (A) Evaluation of SCC between SASA and OH probing based on simulated sequencing depth. (B) Effect of probe size on SCC. (C) Effect of window size on SCC. Second row: Ribose-level SASA (D) Evaluation of SCC between SASA and OH probing based on simulated sequencing depth. (E) Effect of probe size on SCC. (F) Effect of window size on SCC. Third row: Atom-level SASA (G) Evaluation of SCC between SASA and OH probing based on simulated sequencing depth. (H) Effect of probe size on SCC. (I) Effect of window size on SCC.

blocks.text: How FoldBridge should write up RL-Seq

The core rule for RL-Seq within FoldBridge is to treat the hydroxyl-radical solvent-accessibility signal as evidence of backbone accessibility, not as a pairing determination. Raw cleavage counts are first of all provenance: they record how many times a given backbone site was cleaved by •OH under a given cleavage condition. Only when this signal can be interpreted within the chemical context of RL-Seq (backbone solvent exposure, not base pairing) and the readout/mapping context does it enter the default visualisation and confidence.

The key to this boundary is to separate "the break point can be ligation-captured" from "the base is unpaired". Solvent accessible does not equal unpaired: tertiary structure and burial both cause the accessibility of a stretch of backbone to deviate from its secondary structure state. The outside of a paired helix can be highly exposed and cleaved very strongly, while a single strand can be folded and buried with almost no break point left behind. What RL-Seq reads through RtcB ligation is "whether this stretch of backbone faces the solvent", not "whether these bases are paired".

So FoldBridge needs the concept of a gate, rather than a rule code written out of thin air. The responsibility of this gate is: before sending a raw cleavage value into default structure colouring or confidence, first confirm that it satisfies the controlled cleavage conditions (the readout context of Figure 3), confirm that it has been reliably mapped to structural coordinates, and confirm that it is interpreted as backbone solvent accessibility rather than pairing. Only the signal that passes the gate enters the default visual track and the confidence candidate pool; what does not pass is retained as raw provenance, debug information, or download-table fields.

This is not a weakening of RL-Seq, but precisely a respect for its strengths. RL-Seq is very sensitive to backbone solvent accessibility, and the ligation-capture strategy lets it precisely read break points and map onto a real three-dimensional surface; the error occurs only at the moment this strength is expanded into "high cleavage equals unpaired". So for the non-specialist reader, the most robust statement is that "RL-Seq converts the degree of solvent exposure of the backbone, via the ligation capture of the 3′-P break point, into a sequenceable accessibility clue". Spelled out as a complete sentence: in the RL-Seq assay, this backbone site shows a higher solvent accessibility signal; if the cleavage conditions, ligation capture, mapping, coverage, and context all pass the checks, this signal can then serve as support for structural interpretation. It is more verbose than "high signal equals unpaired", but it is more robust scientifically, and more suitable for settling into the long-term knowledge base of FoldBridge.
There is no real FoldBridge accession here, so what follows is a clearly labelled illustrative scenario, used to explain how a single RL-Seq profile is separated into three different layers.

Imagine an RL-Seq profile flowing through the three layers of FoldBridge. The first layer is the raw observation: the count of break points cleaved out by •OH and ligation-captured by RtcB on some stretch of 16S rRNA backbone under controlled cleavage conditions, preserved together with the provenance of cleavage intensity, ligation efficiency, and sequencing. This layer only answers "what was recorded in the raw data", and does not draw conclusions for the reader.

The second layer is the visual signal: only after these break-point counts have been reliably mapped to structural coordinates and interpreted as backbone solvent accessibility do they enter default structure colouring. The colour the reader sees should answer "within the ligation-capture boundary of RL-Seq, what readable accessibility clue is there at this backbone site", not "whether this base is paired". Sites whose cleavage or ligation conditions are unclear, and which have not yet been mapped to reliable coordinates, should not be mixed into this layer to pose as the same kind of evidence.

The third layer is the confidence candidate: only an accessibility signal that has passed the cleavage condition, ligation capture, mapping, and context checks can possibly enter confidence. Even within the same profile, its raw cleavage, its visualised colour, and its confidence are not the same thing.

Running this illustrative profile through is really meant to demonstrate the system behaviour of RL-Seq itself: the ligation-captured break-point counts, the visualisation after colouring, and the final confidence are three different layers, and they must be kept separate. If an explainer article on RL-Seq stirs these three layers together, the mistake the reader is most likely to make is to silently read "the break point can be joined, which shows the backbone faces the solvent" as "this is unpaired".

So the correct ending for an RL-Seq article is not "high cleavage proves this base is unpaired", but: RL-Seq data can provide powerful backbone solvent accessibility clues for RNA structure interpretation; in FoldBridge, such a clue becomes part of structural interpretation and confidence only after it is first interpreted as accessibility (not pairing) and passes the subsequent gates.
